coredata.dc:title: Waste chimney oil to nanolights: A low cost chemosensor for tracer metal detection in practical field and its polymer composite for multidimensional activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Poushali
Ganguly, Sayan
Maity, Priti Prasanna
Bose, Madhuparna
Mondal, Subhadip
Dhara, Santanu
Das, Amit Kumar
Banerjee, Susanta
Das, Narayan Ch.
coredata.dc:description: Proper waste disposal from household and restaurants is becoming an important and recurring waste-management concern. Herein, a method of upcycling of waste kitchen chimney oil has been adopted to prepare fluorescent multifunctional carbon quantum dots. These nanodots showed superior biocompatibility, excellent optical properties, water solubility and high yield. Preparation of C-dots from highly abundant carbon source of waste refusals is highly effective in commercial aspect as well as in reducing the immense environmental pollution. The C-dots showed quasi-spherical size obtained from high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) having an abundance of 1–4 nm in size. The ease of water dispersibility of the nanodots is a mere reflection of their surface polarity which has been supported by Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the field of practical acceptability, the C-dots have been experimented to sense Fe3+ ion in a wide range of concentration (1 nM to 600 μM) with a detection limit of 0.18 nM which can be termed as ‘tracer metal chemosensor’. Moreover, the prepared carbon dots were also tested against inter-cellular Fe3+ ion sensing probe. Lastly, we also fabricate the biopolymer‑carbon dots composite for fluorescent marker ink and light emitting polymer film.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots From Waste Chimney Oil (COC-dots)
Detection of Fe3+ Ion in Environmental Sample
Preparation of Light Emitting Polymer Composite
Synthesis Strategy and Formation Mechanism of COC-dots
Fluorescence Response of the C-dots to the Fe3+ ions
Sensitivity of the Sensor Probe Towards Fe3+ Ions
Evaluation of C-dots Based Fe3+ Sensor in Environmental Samples
Cytotoxicity and In Vito Cell Imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis method of carbon dots (COC-dots) from waste kitchen chimney oil.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum. Inset in (a): photo of COC-dots under sunlight (left) and irradiated by 365 nm (right). (b) Excitation and emission spectra of the aqueous dispersion of COC-dots. (c) Excitation dependent emission spectra of COC-dots excited at different wavelengths. (d) FL decay profile of the COC-dots. (e) FL spectra of COC-dots dispersion in different pH values (from 2 to 11) at 380 nm excitation wavelength. Inset in (e): the reversible pH-responsive photoluminescence behavior of the COC-dots measured by tuning pH from 3 to 10andthen from 10 to 3 (f) Effect of storage time on the FL signal at λex = 380 nm.
(a) TEM image of the COC-dots. (b) Particle size distribution histogram of the COC-dots. Inset in (b): SAED pattern (c) AFM image of the COC-dots. (d) Height profile of the AFM image.

(a) FTIR spectrum (b) NMR of the COC-dots. XPS spectra of (c) wide scan survey of COC-dots. High resolution scans of (d) C1s and (e) O1s spectra (f) S2p spectra for COC-dots nanostructures, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of COC-dots with different concentrations of Fe3+ions. (b) The dependence of F0/F1 on the concentration of Fe3+ ions within the range of 1 nM–600 μM. Inset in (b): Linear plot of the fluorescent response (F0/F1) versus the logarithm of concentrations of Fe3+ions. (c) The selectivity of the sensor and fluorescence response at different metal ions. Inset in (c) photos of COC-dots solution in presence of different metal ions. (d) The selectivity of the sensor towards common anions and organic compounds. The mixture containing Fe3+, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−,Cl−, NO3 −,ascorbic acid, cysteine, glycine, dopamine, ethanol, and methanol.(The concentration of COC-dots, metal ions and these common anions and organic molecules were 2 mg/mL, 50 μM, and 50 μM respectively; Excitation wavelength was 380 nm.)
Schematic approach of fluorescence quenching of COC-dots.

(a) COC-dots-based Fe3+ sensor in determination of environmental water samples: FL response of COC-dots in presence of river water containing different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0 to 300 μM) at λex = 380 nm. (b) The dependence of F0/F1 on the concentration of Fe3+ ions within the range of 5 nM–300 μM. Inset in (b): Linear plot of the fluorescent response (F0/F1) versus the logarithm of concentrations of Fe3+ions.All emission spectra were collected at 380 nm excitation.

(a) Cellular toxicity of the as-prepared COC-dots. Fluorescence microscopic images of MG-63 cells labeled with COC-dots using excitation filters (b) 330–380 nm (blue, DAPI) (c) 450–490 nm (green, Calcein AM) (d) 510–560 nm (red, ETD-1) using ZEN software. Scale bar 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of cell imaging using COC-dots nano-probe and sensing of Fe3+ ions in living cells.

Microscopic images of MG-63 cells first incubated with 2 mg/mL COC-dots for 12 h and then incubated with 5 μM (a–d), 25 μM (e–h), and 50 μM (i–l)of Fe3+ ions, respectively for 1 h at 37 °C. Fluorescence microscopic images of MG-63 cells treated with Fe3+ ions at different excitation filters (b, f, and j)330–380 nm (blue, DAPI), (c, g, and k)) 450–490 nm (green, Calcein AM), and (d, h, and l)) 510–560 nm (red, ETD-1)and (a, e, and i)their corresponding bright-field transmission images of MG-63 cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Appearance of C-dots filled ball point pen refill (a) under visible light and (b) UV light. COC-dots film under (c) daylight and (d) UV light. COC-dots film in state of bending position under (e) daylight and (f) UV light, respectively.

Schematic diagram for the preparation of carbon dots from waste kitchen chimney oil and its application in Fe3+ sensing in (i) aqueous solution, (ii) river water, (iii) living cells, (iv) cell imaging, and versatile application in (v) light emitting polymer composite, (vi) fluorescent ink.
Comparison of carbon dots synthesis from different precursors.

head.ce:title: Waste chimney oil to nanolights: A low cost chemosensor for tracer metal detection in practical field and its polymer composite for multidimensional activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Proper waste disposal from household and restaurants is becoming an important and recurring waste-management concern. Herein, a method of upcycling of waste kitchen chimney oil has been adopted to prepare fluorescent multifunctional carbon quantum dots. These nanodots showed superior biocompatibility, excellent optical properties, water solubility and high yield. Preparation of C-dots from highly abundant carbon source of waste refusals is highly effective in commercial aspect as well as in reducing the immense environmental pollution. The C-dots showed quasi-spherical size obtained from high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) having an abundance of 1–4 nm in size. The ease of water dispersibility of the nanodots is a mere reflection of their surface polarity which has been supported by Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the field of practical acceptability, the C-dots have been experimented to sense Fe3+ ion in a wide range of concentration (1 nM to 600 μM) with a detection limit of 0.18 nM which can be termed as ‘tracer metal chemosensor’. Moreover, the prepared carbon dots were also tested against inter-cellular Fe3+ ion sensing probe. Lastly, we also fabricate the biopolymer‑carbon dots composite for fluorescent marker ink and light emitting polymer film.

ce:section.ce:para: Population outburst, a recurring issue of the generation next civic life is becoming a critical problem throughout the world. If we concentrate to the food consumption, then a least avoidable concern is uptake of edible oils. In order to mitigating the immediate air pollution, the smart civilization coined the technique of prevention of vaporized unused volatile oil during food processing. Thus the collected unused oil is an obvious waste from the household kitchens. Due to lack of disposal of such waste, especially for third world countries, the environmental intoxication (mainly water) cannot be rules out. The present scenario is the dumping of waste oils into drains causes primarily choking of plumbing sites and majorly affects the water, plants and crops, aquatic animals and indirectly the human health [1]. Some techniques for recycling of waste oil are biodiesel production [2], and soap production [3]. Apart from these techniques, are there any other reuse of these waste material? The answer is yes, definitely.

Motivating from the afore-stated issues, waste oil utilization has been accomplished after preparing a special class of colloidal carbon with some unique attributes. These colloidal carbons are specialty class of nanocarbons which are known as carbon dots (C-dots). C-dots are special class of carbonaceous quasi-spherical nanomaterials having diameters below 10 nm. The critical size, unique optical features (excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) character, easy of processing and fast water dispersability make these novel nano carbons a high selective chemosensor and non-photobleachable bio-marker [4,5]. C-dots are serendipitous production by a group of researchers during purification of single wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) [6]. After that path-breaking finding there were several experiments went throughout the broad range of scientific researches. The researches has been scattered in high-flux membrane technology [7,8], sorption technologies [9], bactericidal and bacteriostatic applications [10,11], environmental sensors [12,13], renewable energy harvesting [14,15], bio sensing [16,17], bio-imaging [18,19], drug delivery [20], light emitting diode (LED) [21], catalytic activity [22,23], and storage applications [24].

Common preparation routes for these C-dots can be classified as top down and bottom up approach. Between them bottom up approach is more versatile due to simplicity in synthesis. Bottom up approach consists of thermal heating/coupling [25], synthetic support [26] and microwave irradiation [27]. A few years back researchers took new pathway to prepare C-dots in single step by adopting the green chemistry route [28]. Such routes have more acceptances because of their low cost productive techniques, fast synthesis, less hazardous in purification and high yield. Fluorescent C-dots was prepared by Zou's groups using watermelon peel as a waste raw source of carbons which was later used as bio imaging probe [29]. Liu and his coworkers also prepared C-dots from pomelo peel as carbon source to sense mercury in water. They also did work on flour as the carbon source to prepare C-dots via microwave irradiation [30]. There were also various researches on easy synthesis of C-dots e.g. apple juice [31], coffee grounds [32], gelatin [33], candle soot [34], sugarcane bagasse pulp [35], tomato [36], neem gum [37] etc.

In this work, we choose waste oil from kitchen chimney as precursor material for the robust wet synthesis technique of C-dots.by using conc. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4). Owing to the strong oxidizing and dehydrating features, conc. H2SO4 has been applied for the synthesis of C-dots to carbonize various carbon sources viz. carbonization of hair fiber [38], dehydration of carbohydrates [39]. These approaches are complicated and cost intensive process because of the consumption of large amount of acid, synthesis duration and manual labour. These drawbacks restrict the immense use of C-dots for large-scale production. Compare to these previous techniques, our method for preparation has been done in one step within 10 min. The present approach offers utilization of waste in the fabrication of value-added products which decreases environmental pollution, and provides a solution to waste management. These C-dots revealed high stability, bright cyan fluorescence under UV light with narrow size distribution, biocompatibility, and fascinating optical properties. As a selective tracer metal sensing nanoprobe, the sensing activity has been also evaluated in environmental simulated aqueous samples based on Fe3+ ions. The ultra-low detection limit (0.18 nM) made these tiny nanoparticles suitable for Fe3+ detection in environmental water. The photo-quenching behaviour was also observed in practical specimens (here river water) which supports the potential application of the prepared C-dots as nano-chemosensor. The prepared C-dots can easily form gel by mixing with a biopolymer, chitosan; which can be further used for filling up refill of a ball-point pen for UV-active marking. Also, C-dots were used for the fabrication of flexible light emitting polymer composite with PVP. Further, these C-dots shows high intracellular delivery efficiency and sensitivity to Fe3+ ion even present in complicated system, e.g. cell medium.
Kitchen chimney waste oil was obtained from the local household. Concentrated sulfuric acid (99.9%), quinine sulfate (>99.0%) and 3-(4,5-Dime-thylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium.

bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. CuSO4·5H2O, HgO, FeSO4·7H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, FeCl3, AgNO3, CdCl2, BaCl2.2H2O, (CH3COO)2Zn2·H2O, NaCl, KCl, MnCl2, (CH3COO)2Pb·3H2O, CoCl2, NiCl2, and AlCl3 were purchased from Merck, Germany. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots From Waste Chimney Oil (COC-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots were prepared by simple ultrasonication of waste kitchen chimney oil with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric acid. In a typical process, 6 mL of kitchen chimney waste oil was added into 50 mL beaker and heated to 100 °C. Afterward, 1 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (99.9%) was added dropwise under ultrasonication for 10 min. Then the mixture was diluted with 40 mL deionized water and the pH was adjusted to 7 using NaOH. The suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove large carbonaceous residues, yielding a yellowish clear dispersion of C-dots. Purification of the carbon dots was conducted through dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off: 1000 Da) for 3 days. The deionized water was changed every 6 h. Finally, 1.127 g of purified C-dots was obtained. The synthetic method and multifunctional applications of COC-dots was represented in Scheme 1 .

The quantum yield of the as-prepared COC-dots is measured by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities and absorbance values against the reference quinine sulfate. The quantum yield of quinine sulfate is reported to be 54% in the literature when dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33). The quantum yield of the C-dots can be calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q x
=
Q std
I x
/
A x
]
A std
/
I std
[
η 2
x
/
η 2
std
where Q represents the quantum yield, the subscript “x” refers to the unknown quantum yield of the C-dots. I correspond to the measured integrated emission intensity. The subscript “std” refers to the standard reference, η is the refractive index and A represents the optical density, which is quantified via a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The detection experiment of Fe3+ ions was performed in aqueous solution at room temperature. In a typical assay, 100 μL of COC-dots (10 mg/mL) dispersion was diluted to 2 mL with PBS buffer solution. After that, different concentrations of Fe3+ solution were added to the C-dots and mixed thoroughly. The fluorescence measurements were performed after reaction for 3 min.

The selectivity of the detection system was confirmed by measuring the fluorescence spectra of other metal ions such as Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Al3+ in the same way. Moreover, to explore the effect of some organic compound and common anions on the Fe3+ sensing including ascorbic acid, dopamine, methanol, glycine, ethanol, cysteine, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 3− were chosen for the experiment. The concentrations of these species were 50 μM and all the fluorescence measurement was recorded at 380 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ Ion in Environmental Sample
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples were obtained from Ganga River, West Bengal, India. Prior to detection, the water samples were filtered twice using a 0.22 μm membrane and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min to remove impurities and large solids. After that, to the 2 mL water aliquots 100 μL of COC-dots were added and were spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 380 nm excitation.

In generally, quenching mechanism can be described by both (a) dynamic quenching and (b) static quenching. Dynamic quenching originated from the collisional interaction between the fluorophore and the quencher molecule. For dynamic quenching the Stern-Volmer equation can be described as follows [40]
(2)
F 0
/ F =
τ 0
/ τ = 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
where, F 0 and F are the fluorescent intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher molecule, respectively. τ 0 and τ are the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of the quencher molecule, respectively. k q and [Q] represents the rate constant of dynamic (collisional) quenching and concentration of the quencher molecule, respectively. Another kind of quenching, i.e. static quenching occurs when a non-fluorescent complex compound produced between the quencher and the fluorophore. This type of quenching can be described by the following Stern-Volmer equation
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where, K SV is the Stern-Volmer constant.

The cytotoxicity of COC-dots was evaluated by using MTT assay. MG-63 cells were grown in high glucose DMEM media (Himedia) with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotic solution in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C,(Heracell 150i, Thermo, USA). The cells were harvested from tissue culture flasks using 0.25% trypsin with1 mM EDTA (Himedia). Cell density was counted and plated equal volume on samples and tissue culture plate (TCP) as a control in 24 well plate. Cells were treated with different doses of COC-dots solutions (0, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, and 700 μg/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. After incubation, the attached MG-63 cells in 96 well plates were treated with freshly prepared methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5 mg/mL) and incubated again for 4 h to allow formation of violet-colored formazan. The supernatant solution was carefully removed and DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals formed from the reduction of MTT by the active cells. Absorbance was measured using a microplate reader (Bio-RAD 680, USA) at reference filter at 570 nm. The optical density was correlated with the cell viability, under the assumption of 100% viability for the control sample without any COC-dots.

For in vitro cell imaging, the MG-63 cells were cultured for 24 h in 24-well plate with complete high glucose DMEM media (Himedia) at 37 °C with 5% CO2 atmosphere. After 24 h culture, the DMEM media was replaced and cells were incubated (37 °C with 5% CO2) with 270 μL of DMEM containing 100 μL of COC-dots (5 mg/mL). The culture media was removed followed by washing thrice with PBS to remove the remaining C-dots. The cells were observed using a fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) with ZEN software. For the intracellular Fe3+ detection experiment, 35 μL of Fe3+ at different doses (5 μL, 25 μL, and 50 μL) were added to the COC-dots pretreated MG-63 cells. After that, the cells were mounted on microscopic slides with glycerol and were observed using fluorescence microscope by the above describe method.

For the preparation of fluorescent ink, 100 mg chitosan was added to 12 mL of water in assistance with 0.1 M acetic acid followed by vigorous stirring until it get dissolved to turn a transparent homogeneous solution. After that, desired amount of COC-dots was added to the resultant solution and stirred for 5 min. The fluorescent ink was filled into an ordinary ball-point pen refill and it was ready for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Light Emitting Polymer Composite
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 0.2 g of PVP was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water at 60 °C until a clear solution was achieved. 800 μL of C-dots solution (10 mg/mL) was added drop wise to PVP solution and stirred gently for 30 min. The mixed solution was then subjected to centrifuge at 10000 rpm for 15 min under ambient condition for degasification. Lastly, the resultant solution was casted into a petri dish with side to side vibration to make the solution well distributed and dried in an oven at 120 °C.

The UV–visible spectrum of COC-dots in aqueous solution was measured using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Singapore PTE Ltd., Model lambda 35). The FTIR spectrum of COC-dots was recorded on a FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, model-Spectrum-2, Singapore) in ATR mode with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 16 scans in the wavelength range of 500–4000 cm−1 at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiment was performed in Bruker, D2 phaser with SSD160 detector. The morphology and the microstructure were obtained by high resolution transmission electron microscope, HRTEM (JEOL JEM 2100, Japan; operating voltage 200 kV with filament LaB6). The elemental composition was analyzed electron dispersive X-ray study (EDX, INCA PentaFET x3, Oxford Instrument UK). Surface topography of COC-dots was scanned by Agilent 5500 scanning probe microscope (force constant was kept at 48 Nm−1 and resonance frequency of tip was 146–236 kHz) after drop casting of COC-dots on Silicon wafer as substrate. The images were taken in non-contact tapping mode with a scan area of 500 × 500 nm2. Using tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal standard 1H NMR was measured in on a Bruker, 600 MHz instrument at ambient temperature. Fluorescence measurements were performed on aFluoromax_4C_1052D_4312_FM spectrofluorometer in aqueous solution. The fluorescence lifetime was measured at room temperature using a diode laser (Model EPL 375, Edinburg Instruments, Life Spec II) of wavelength 376.6 nm. Raman measurements were performed using spectrometer (MODEL T64000 (Make JobinYvon Horiba, France). Argon-Krypton mixed ion gas laser (MODEL 2018 RM) was used as an excitation source. Thermoelectric cooled front illuminated 1024 256 CCD, MODELSynpseTM (Make JobinYvon Horiba, France) was used as the detector. Fluorescent microscopic images were taken on Carl Zeiss, Germany using ZEN software. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with a VG Scientific ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (UK) equipped with a Mg Kα excitation source (1253.6 eV) and a fivechanneltron detection system. The zeta potential was recorded on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Manchester, U.K.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis Strategy and Formation Mechanism of COC-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Waste oil derived from kitchen chimney mostly constitutes of triglycerides [2] which is hydrophobic in nature and insoluble in water. It is critical to convert hydrophobic waste oil into hydrophilic zero dimensional C-dots containing unsaturated sp2 carbon clusters. As a carbonization/oxidizing agent concentrated H2SO4 was chosen due to the following reasons: (a) concentrated H2SO4 has strong dehydrating property which assists the generation of unsaturated CC from saturated CC and (b) the strong oxidizing property of concentrated H2SO4 helps the formation of hydrophilic OCOH and COH from hydrophobic CH.

The acid treatment was assisted with ultrasonication. The detailed mechanism under ultrasonication can be contemplated as follows: waste oil in the form of triglycerides treated with concentrated H2SO4 to form C-dots under irradiation of ultrasonic waves. In comparison to traditional energy sources, ultrasonic irradiation affords extremely high localized temperatures and pressures (unusual reaction condition) in liquids within a short period of time that cannot be obtained by other methods. Common bath sonicators have the power intensity of ~106 Jm−2. The power intensity has the most significant effect to turn the precursor mass into a stable colloidal dispersion without any premature settling out. The incident acoustic wavelengths are larger than molecular dimensions, and therefore direct molecular level interaction between the chemical species and ultrasound does not take place. Acoustic cavitation i.e. the formation, nucleation/growth, and implosive collapse of bubbles in liquid environment driven by high intensity ultrasound accounts for the chemical effects of ultrasound. Collapsing of bubbles evolves intense thermal energy and pressure within a very short duration which has a quite resemblance to the sudden bursting effect inside the working liquid media. According to some previous studies it was documented that sonication method evolves ~5000 K temperature, ~1000 bar pressure and high cooling rate of >1010 K/s. Ultrasonic waves are efficient enough to produce alternating rarefactions and compressions in liquid and hence causes in implosive and violent collapse of small vacuum bubbles [41], which leads to the generation of cavitations during compression. Such an implosion causes enhanced hydrodynamic shear stress in the liquid resulting breakdown of the macro-particles. Thereafter, the intermediates formed undergo carbonization leading to the synthesis of chimney oil derived carbon dots (COC-dots). The photoluminescence property of such solutions was enhanced when treated with NaOH solution, which introduces more surface defects in the sp2 carbon framework that leads to improved emissive traps and supporting augmentation of fluorescence. The yield of the reaction was 24.1% with respect to precursors which is comparable with the studies reported elsewhere [38,42]. The entire schematic illustration for the adopted technique has been showed in Fig. 1 .

The aqueous dispersion of COC-dots displayed two characteristic absorption peaks (Fig. 2a) at 263 and 325 nm. The absorption peak at 263 nm was associated with a typical absorption of a sp2 carbon network, which is reminiscent of an aromatic system. The peak at 325 nm was related to the n to π* transition of the carbonyl functional group present at the surface of the C-dots.[43]The diluted aqueous solution of the COC-dots is slight yellow under natural light, however it emits vivid cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV light excitation. The as-prepared COC-dots show a maximum emission at 472 nm upon excitation at 380 nm (Fig. 2b). The emission spectra of the C-dots unveiled an excitation dependent behavior, which is quite dissimilar from that of organic dyes and other semiconductor quantum dots as demonstrated in Fig. 2c. The maximum emission peak of the COC-dots shifts from 418 nm to 508 nm when excitation wavelength (λex) varied from 320 nm to 420 nm. This feature enables the multicolor fluorescence which arises not only from the size distribution but also from the distribution of different emissive trap sites [44]. The multicolor luminescence feature of the COC-dots is the reason of heterogeneity of C-dots comprising chromophores at different energy levels is reported elsewhere [45]. The average fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 2d) was determined to be an average value of 4.08 ns, which is appropriate for biological applications [46]. Using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference, the quantum yield of the COC-dots was measured as 7.5%, which is higher than the value of previously reported studies tabulated in Table 1 .

The optical stability of the COC-dots was investigated under different environments. No noticeable decline of fluorescence (FL) intensity was observed in high concentration of KCl or NaCl solution (Fig. S1). The COC-dots show high stability at room temperature. No apparent photobleaching loss under continuous irradiation with UV lamp for 4 h (Fig. S2) was observed. These results are suggesting their advantages in potential application fields. The FL intensities of the COC-dots solution at different pH solutions (2−11) were measured. In comparison to the most pH-insensitive C-dots, our as-prepared COC-dots exhibit pH-sensitive property. Fig. 1e demonstrated that the FL intensity of the C-dots increases as the pH value increases step by step in the range 2–11. Interestingly, pH responsive FL intensity of the COC-dots is reversible. To evaluate the sustainability in pH-reversible environment, the C-dots were subjected to keep in contact with acid and basic media in a cyclic order. When the pH is switched from 3 to 9 after addition of aqueous NaOH, the FL intensity increases and decreases back to the initial value after addition of HCl. Even after ten cycles from pH 3–10, a noteworthy pH-switching luminescence property was noticed with no visible perturbation (inset of Fig. 1e). This performance is assigned to the different degrees of protonation of the COC-dots at different pH and can be illustrated through protonation and deprotonation mechanism [55,56]. The degree of deprotonation gradually increases with increasing pH, leading to higher amount of carboxyl functional groups at the surface of the COC-dots which works as fluorophore species and consequently evokes strong FL intensity. The high optical stability and the pH sensitive feature of the COC-dots make them attractive fluorescent probes for pH-monitoring in biomedical and biological field. Also any type of floating or precipitated particles was not found even after stored for 60 days in air (Fig. 1f). Additionally, we examined the COC-dots under different conditions. The FL intensity of COC-dots hardly changed by different physiological conditions (Fig. S3a) such as pH 7.4, 10 mM tris-HCl, 0.15 mM NaCl. A beam of red light was illuminated in the COC-dots aqueous solution to verify the dispersibility. The Tyndall effect was clearly observed in Fig. S3b. The overall results reveal that the as-prepared COC-dots exhibit good dispersibility and promising photoluminescent properties in aqueous solution. Furthermore, the as-synthesized COC-dots display good stability even in a complicated system such a blood serum. The stability data was confirmed with three parallel blood samples (Fig. S4).

The size and morphology of the COC-dots were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The TEM images (Fig. 3a) revealed that the C-dots were spherical in shape with an average diameter of 2.5 nm. The TEM result showed that COC-dots have relatively small size distribution and based on the statistical analysis of 100 random particles with the majority of the particles are falling within 1–4 nm (Fig. 3b).The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image (inset of Fig. 3b) showed the amorphous nature of the COC-dots. The AFM image (Fig. 3c) also supports the spherical morphology and the depth profile confirms the size distribution of COC-dots (Fig. 3d).

To further elucidate the formation mechanism of COC-dots, we characterized the C-dots using FTIR, XPS, and NMR spectroscopy. FTIR spectroscopy was performed to characterize the chemical functional groups on the surface of the C-dots. The as-synthesized COC-dots disclosed (Fig. 4a) peaks at 1732 cm−1(CO stretching), 1111 cm−1(SO/CO stretching), 3512 cm−1 (OH stretching), 2937, and 2849 cm−1 (CH stretching). Peaks at 1634 and 1389 cm−1 were assigned to CC and COO– stretching, respectively. The existence of out-of-plane bending vibrations of benzene CH located at around 768 cm−1 indicates the presence of the aromatic rings on the surface of the COC-dots [12]. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 4b) two different types of chemical environments of protons were observed: (a) 1–2.3 ppm for sp3 CH protons, and (b) 3.4–4.3 ppm for the protons attached with the ether, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups [57]. In accordance with the results of the FTIR and 1H NMR, high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra further confirmed the surface passivation and elemental composition of the C-dots. Two noticeable features of C1s and O1s associated with two small peaks at S2s and S2p are visible in the survey spectrum (Fig. 4c). The survey spectrum indicates that the C-dots are basically composed of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) as well as limited amount of sulfur (S) which may come from chimney derived oil and sulfuric acid. The C1s spectrum shows five different carbon states at 284.0, 284.6, 285.4, 286.7, 289.8 eV for CC, CC, CS, CO, OCOH, respectively (Fig. 4d) [58]. The O1s spectrum of the C-dots exhibits two signals of CO at 533.3 and CO at 531.5 eV, respectively shown in Fig. 4e [48]. In the S2p spectrum (Fig. 4f) two distinct peaks at 163.4 and 167.5 eV indicate two forms of sulfur. The former peak located at 163.4 eV can be deconvoluted into two different components at 163.0 and 164.2 eV, which assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of thiophene‑sulfur due to their spin orbit coupling.[38,59]The latter peak resolved into three different components at 169.0, 168.3, 167.2 eV corresponding to some oxidized form of sulfur for instance sulfonate.[38]The above results suggest that in the carbon framework of the COC-dots the S atoms have not only doped in the form of thiophene, but also the surface of the C-dots was functionalized in the form of sulfonate.

The aqueous solution of the COC-dots shows zeta potential of about −56.2 mV. Such a high value indicates that they are negatively charged owing to the presence of plenty of carboxylic and sulfonic groups on the surface of the C-dots. The C-dots obtained from waste oil are very much prone to soluble in hydrophilic solvents such as N,N-dimethylformamide, ethanol but poorly soluble in hydrophobic solvents e.g. cyclohexane and ethyl acetate although they comprise of ample of aliphatic groups. This result suggests that the hydrophobic aliphatic groups mostly present in the core of the C-dots, whereas the hydrophilic groups exist at the surface of the C-dots. Altogether, a brand new type C-dots was successfully prepared through a one step and simple method. The as-prepared COC-dots are carbon-rich, sulfur doped, functionalized with ample of hydrophilic groups at the surface and aliphatic and aromatic carbons in the core, we asked whether these COC-dots could potentially serve as fluorescent platform to sense Fe3+ ions or not [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Response of the C-dots to the Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Towards that purpose, Fe3+ ions were introduced to the prepared COC-dots solution. Fig. S5 depicted the UV–vis absorption spectra of the COC-dots in presence of Fe3+ ion. Fe3+ aqueous solution showed a strong absorption peak at around 302 nm. The addition of 100 μM Fe3+ ions to the C-dots solution results in disappearance of the absorption peak for Fe3+ion. This result indicates that reaction had occurred between COC-dots and Fe3+ ions. The FL spectrum of the C-dots was represented in Fig. S6 at excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The COC-dots disclosed strong FL intensity and upon addition of 150 μM Fe3+ ions the FL intensity drastically decreased. However, the increment of the quenching efficiency was observed upon increasing the Fe3+ concentration. The digital photograph (Fig. S6 inset) of the COC-dots exhibited bright cyan fluorescence under UV exposure which was quenched after the addition of Fe3+ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the Sensor Probe Towards Fe3+ Ions
ce:section.ce:para: To get best detection result, the influence of reaction time for reaction equilibrium and the reaction temperature on the sensing ability of the COC-dots was optimized. The aqueous dispersion of the COC-dots showed stable FL intensity. Fig. S7 revealed the time dependent FL spectra of the COC-dots in presence of Fe3+ ion. Upon addition of 150 μM Fe3+ ion, the emission intensity of the COC-dots was decreased with time due to the quenching in presence of Fe3+ and the FL signal became stable after 3 min. The result suggests that 3 min reaction time required to reach the reaction equilibrium between COC-dots and Fe3+. The effect of reaction temperature in the range of 30–60 °C were studied with 150 μM Fe3+ ion, suggesting that the FL intensity of the COC-dots were independent of the reaction temperature (Fig. S8)·We evaluated the ability of the sensor probe for the quantification of Fe3+ ions. Different concentrations of Fe3+solution ranging from 0 to 600 μM were studied. In a typical run, aqueous dispersion of COC-dots was added into calculated amount of various concentration of Fe3+ solution. The FL spectra were analyzed after reaction of 3 min at room temperature. Fig. 5a displays the FL spectra of the COC-dots at different concentration of Fe3+ solution, demonstrating that FL intensity is sensitive to Fe3+ concentration. The FL intensity of the COC-dots was decreased (Fig. 5a) gradually with the increasing concentration of the Fe3+ ions when excited at 380 nm. The result provides the feasibility of the Fe3+ sensing. Fig. 5b shows the relationship between FL intensity and Fe3+ concentration. The plot does not fit a conventional linear Stern-Volmer equation which indicates both static and dynamic quenching processes occur in this sensor probe [61]. A good linear correlation (inset of Fig. 5b) can be observed between the quenching efficiency (F0/F1) and the logarithm of Fe3+concentrationin the range of 1 nm to 5 μM. Moreover, we have performed the lifetime measurement of COC-dots in presence of Fe3+. The result (Fig. S9) shows a much lower lifetime (1.02 ns) than COC-dots. This decrement in FL lifetime suggests that reaction has occurred between C-dots and Fe3+ which results in FL quenching. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.18 nM using the three times standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the calibration curve). This detection limit (0.18 nM) is more sensitive than the other reported COC-dots based detection methods (shown in Table S1) [62]. The results revealed that this sensor probe is feasible for the detection of trace amount of Fe3+. Moreover, in most of the cases the C-dots based sensor probe for Fe3+ detection did not show their applicability in real samples (Table S1). In view of the significant photostability and high sensitivity of the C-dots, it is possible to apply this sensor probe for sensing Fe3+ in real samples.

Fig. 5c represents the remarkable selectivity of the nano-sensor towards common metal ions (including Pb2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Na+, K+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, and Al3+). In addition to metal ions different organic molecules and anions may exist with Fe3+ in environmental samples. Therefore, we examined the influence of different anions and organic molecules on the detection of Fe3+ ions. As depicted in Fig. 5d, in the presence of interfering species including CO3 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Cl−, NO3 −, ethanol, methanol, cysteine, glycine, and dopamine, only slight interference was found. The above mentioned results confirmed that the proposed method have excellent selectivity towards Fe3+ ions.

The high selectivity of the COC-dots towards the Fe3+ ion could be attributed to the strong interaction between COC-dots and Fe3+ ion. XPS results demonstrated that oxygen is main component of the C-dots compare to sulfur. According to FTIR data, the surface of the COC-dots was functionalized with ample of hydroxyl groups. Furthermore, the oxygen containing groups have higher electronegativity than the sulfur containing groups [63]. Therefore, we proposed that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was mostly ascribed to the binding affinity of the hydroxyl group present on the surface of the COC-dots to the Fe3+ion. It is well known that the phenolic hydroxyl groups can form complex with Fe3+ ion. Hence, the hydroxyl groups of the COC-dots would react with Fe3+ ion to form complex through coordination reaction [64]. The as-formed Fe-C-dots complex would promote charge transfer and therefore restrain exciton recombination which leads to remarkable fluorescence quenching. The schematic of fluorescence quenching is represented in Fig. 6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of C-dots Based Fe3+ Sensor in Environmental Samples
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Table S1, the COC-dots based Fe3+ sensors does not show application in environmental water samples. To investigate the utilization of the as-prepared COC-dots in real samples the practical application of the sensing probe was carried out using water collected from Ganga River. Prior to detection, the river water samples were purified by simple filtration using filtration membrane with 0.22 μm pore size and centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 30 min. The water samples were then spiked with different concentration of Fe3+ and then evaluated with the proposed method. The FL signal (Fig. 7a) of the COC-dots in the presence of river water containing Fe3+ ions dramatically decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration (5 nM to 100 μM). Fig. 7b represents the dependence of F 0/F on the concentration of Fe3+ ion over the range 0 to 100 μM. The corresponding linear relationship was shown in the inset of Fig. 7b In spite of the interference of different minerals, domestic sewage and organics present in the river water, no apparent disturbing effect was found on the sensing capacity of the COC-dots. This robust sensing probe can still perform for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and In Vito Cell Imaging
ce:section.ce:para: From the detailed characterizations mentioned above, the prepared COC-dots benefits of small particle size, excellent photostability, high selectivity, superior photostability, excellent ionic strength. Due to these magnificent properties of the COC-dots we investigate whether these nanoparticles are suitable for biological applications. Therefore, MTT assays were carried out to study the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared COC-dots. Here, MG-63 cells were chosen as a model cell line for the experiment. The cell viability experiment (Fig. 8a) showed that addition of COC-dots over a wide range of concentrations from 0 to 700 μg/mL the cells viability was estimated to be >90%. The high cell viability suggests that the COC-dots have low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and potential for monitoring Fe3+ in living cells. Fig. 8b–d displays MG-63 cells after incubated with COC-dots (2 mg/mL) at 37οC for 12 h. Bright fluorescence emission from the intracellular region could be observed in the cytoplasm of the MG-63 cells which suggests that via endocytosis process the COC-dots have efficiently entered into the cells. The cells revealed bright blue (Fig. 8b), green (Fig. 8c) and red emission (Fig. 8d) under different exciter filters in the fluorescence mode. The excitation-dependent FL behavior of the C-dots made it feasible for the multiple color emission in the MG-63 cells imaged by using COC-dots only.

To further examine if our fluorescent nano-probe could be applied for semiquantitative monitoring of Fe3+ in living cells, exogenous Fe3+ ion was added into the COC-dots pretreated MG-63 cells. The schematic diagram of cell imaging and intracellular fluorescence imaging is depicted in Fig. 9 . For the experiment MG-63 cells were supplemented with different concentrations of the Fe3+ (5–50 μM) in growth medium and incubated for 60 min at 37οC. The fluorescence microscopic images showed very feeble intracellular fluorescence. When the concentration of Fe3+ was 5 μM, the quenching of the FL intensity can be observed which ascribed to that Fe3+ ions have entered into the cells (Fig. 10b, c, and d). The FL intensity of the COC-dots substantially quenched (Fig. 10f, g, and h) when the Fe3+ concentration reached to 25 μM. Almost complete disappearance of FL intensity was observed at 50 μM Fe3+concentration which indicate that the high concentration of Fe3+ caused almost complete quenching of the COC-dots (Fig. 10j, k, and l). The above-mentioned observation affirms that waste oil derived C-dots have the capability of highly sensitive monitoring and visualization of Fe3+ ion in living cells. In this context it is important to mention that the sensing speed and sensitivity was lower than in the aqueous solution. This is probably because of the fact that the inner and outer membranes of the cells have high adsorption capacity of Fe3+ ion.

COC-dots have been employed for versatile application including UV-active ink, which is undetectable under visible light and light emitting polymer composite. For this purpose, fluorescent gel was prepared using chitosan and the as-prepared COC-dots. The ink was filled in a ball point pen and the image was taken under visible light (Fig. 11a) and UV light (Fig. 11b). The images display that under UV light the ink exhibits bright cyan fluorescent whereas under visible light it is transparent. Fig. 11c–f shows the images of light emitting polymer composite film under visible light and UV light. PVP/COC-dots polymer composite film shows bright fluorescence under UV light whereas the film is almost colorless and highly transparent in visible light. This type of film can be further applicable as sunlight conversion film. Considering the robust performance of the fluorescent COC-dots, the present work discloses enormous prospect to bring COC-dots from experimental research to practical applications.

In summary, we have developed a simple method for the preparation of highly stable and biocompatible C-dots from the waste kitchen chimney oil for the first time. The multifunctionality of COC-dots has been established in their versatile area of applications in novel sensing probe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution as well as in environmental samples even at very low concentration. The sensing mechanism for the COC-dots could be ascribed to the formation of the complexes between the Fe3+ ion and the surface hydroxyl groups of the carbon nanodots. Further, the COC-dots were effectively applied to the detection of Fe3+ in river water and living cells. The versatile application of fluorescent ink and light emitting polymer composite can be applied to UV-active marker and sunlight conversion film. The present study demonstrates a cost effective and simple route towards the fabrication of C-dots for sensing, bio-labeling, light emitting nano-composite and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2018.01.019.
